concepts.title: Red phosphorus
concepts.conceptBody:
Red Phosphorus

Red phosporus is prepared by heating white phosphorus in the absence of air at $250\text{C}$ for a few hours with a trace of iodine as catalyst in a closed iron vessel fitted with a safety valve. At the end of conversion, the product is ground under water and treated with hot caustic soda solution to eliminate any unchanged white phosphorus. It is finally washed with hot water and dried by heating with steam.

Another allotrope of phosphorus is black phosphorus and is obtained by heating white phosphorus at $470~\text{K}$ under high pressure. A series of phases of black phosphorus are formed and one of these phases consists of an extended layer structure in which each phosphorus is bonded to three neighbours by single covalent bonds. Thermodynamically, black phosphorus is the most stable form.

White phosphorus, the form produced in the industrial synthesis, is a toxic, waxy, white solid that contains discrete tetrahedral ${{\text{P}}_{4}}$ molecules. Red phosphorus, by contrast, is essentially nontoxic and has a polymeric structure:

As expected for a molecular solid that contains small, non-polar molecules, white phosphorus has a low melting point, $(44\text{C})$ and is soluble in non-polar solvent such as $\text{C}{{\text{S}}_{2}}$. It is highly reactive, bursting into flames when exposed to air, and is thus stored under water .

concepts.title: Phospine
concepts.conceptBody:
Phosphine $\left( \mathbf{P}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{3}}} \right)$

(i) When a mixture of white $\text{P}\left( {{\text{P}}_{4}} \right)$ and $\text{NaOH}$ soln. is heated, $\text{P}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ is formed.

${{\text{P}}_{4}}+2\text{NaOH}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{\Delta }3\text{Na}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+\text{P}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$

In this reaction some ${{\text{P}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ is also formed as side product. It is inflammable in air.

(ii) Ionic phosphides react with water to form $\text{P}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$.

$\text{C}{{\text{a}}_{3}}{{\text{P}}_{2}}+6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}3\text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+2\text{P}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$

$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ is highly soluble in water by $\text{P}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ is insoluble in water. $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ dissolves due to H-bonding.

$\text{P}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ cannot form $\text{H}$-bond, so it is insoluble in water.

concepts.title: Oxides of phosphorus
concepts.conceptBody:
Oxides of Phosphorus

$\mathbf{P-oxides }{{\mathbf{P}}_{\mathbf{4}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{6}}}\mathbf{ and }{{\mathbf{P}}_{\mathbf{4}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{10}}}$

${{\left( {{\text{P}}_{4}} \right)}_{\text{X}}}\xrightarrow[600{}^\circ C]{Stream\,\,of\,\,air}{{\text{P}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{6}}\text{ }$

${{\text{P}}_{4}}\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{Excess\,\,of\,\,air}{{\text{P}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{10}}$

These oxides are solids.

They differ from $\text{N}$ oxides in stoichiometry and structure. It is due to inbality of $\text{P}$ to form $(\text{p}-\text{p})\pi $ bonds.

${{\text{P}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{6}}$ and ${{\text{P}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{10}}$ both have cage structure. The structures are based upon ${{\text{P}}_{4}}$ tetrahedra.

${{\mathbf{P}}_{4}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{6}}$

In this oxide $\text{P}$ - atoms form a regular tetrahedron and $\text{O}$ - atoms are present at six edges of the tetrahedron and bridge $\text{P}-$ atoms.

${{\mathbf{P}}_{\mathbf{4}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{10}}}$

In this oxide ${{\text{P}}_{4}}$ tetrahedron has six edge-bridging O-atoms and four terminal O-atoms. The terminal $\text{P}-\text{O}$ bonds have $(\text{d}-\text{p})\pi $ bonding. Therefore, there are two types of $\text{P}-\text{O}$ distances in ${{\text{P}}_{4}}{{\text{O}}_{10}}$ large bridging $\text{P}-\text{O}$ and small terminal $\text{P}-\text{O}$ distances.

concepts.title: Oxy acids of P and phosphorus Halides
concepts.conceptBody:
Oxy acids of P

The common oxy acids of $P$ are:

${{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{220{}^\circ C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}{{\text{P}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}\xrightarrow{320{}^\circ C}\left( \text{HP}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)$

Phosphorus Halides

${{\text{P}}_{4}}+6{{\text{X}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}4\text{P}{{\text{X}}_{3}}\text{ (in limited supply of }{{\text{X}}_{2}}\text{ )}$

${{\text{P}}_{4}}+10{{\text{X}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}4\text{P}{{\text{X}}_{5}}\text{ (in excess amount of }{{\text{X}}_{2}}\text{ ) }$

  • $\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{POC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$
  • $\text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$
  • $\text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+\text{HI}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{P}{{\text{I}}_{3}}+3\text{HCl}$
  • $3\text{ROH}+\text{PCl}\xrightarrow{{}}3\text{RCl}+{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$
  • $\text{ROH}+\text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{5}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{RCl}+\text{POC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+\text{HCl}$
